content:
Devic
Devic
CMPSC 311 - Introduction 
to Systems Programming
Dynamic Memory
“You are now all sanitation engineers”
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Dynamic Memory Allocation
• What we want is dynamically allocated memory
• We want to explicitly request a new block of memory
• But how? 
• The language runtime allocates it, perhaps with help from OS
• Dynamically allocated memory persists until:
• your code explicitly deallocates it [manual memory management]
• C, C++, Rust
• a garbage collector collects it [automatic memory management]
• Java, Python, Go
• C requires you to manually manage dynamic memory
• gives you more control and performance, but can cause headaches
• C has no garbage collection. YOU ARE THE GARBAGE COLLECTOR
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Aside: Garbage Collection, in general
• In some languages like Java, you can dynamically allocate objects using the 
built-in “new” function of the Java runtime environment
• Stays in memory until an invisible process behind the scenes frees.
• This invisible process is called garbage collection
• Typically done in the background by a background process
• Tracks scope, use, reference, and other metadata to know when to reap objects
• Pros: a large class of memory bugs avoided
• dereferencing dangling pointers, memory leaks, double freeing of memory, …
• Cons: unpredictable performance
• garbage collection can start at an arbitrary time and slow down the program*
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
String str1 = new String(“This is a text string”);

Devic
Devic
Dynamic Memory Allocation
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
0x00000000
0xFFFFFFFF
OS kernel [protected]
stack (automatic)
read/write segment
.data, .bss (static)
read-only segment
.text, .rodata (static)
• Remember, we want dynamic memory to allow us to:
• … allocate an arbitrary number of bytes
• … use and release (or “free”) memory at any point
• Non-static, non-automatic
• … not interfere with our existing segments
• Given these wants, where do we place said data?

Devic
Devic
The Heap
• The heap is a segment in virtual memory that:
• holds a large pool of unused memory that is used for 
dynamically allocated data
• Unordered…
• Allocating new memory doesn’t necessarily mean new 
memory will be “above” or “before” other dynamic memory
• …but structured (usually)
• Most libraries inject hidden metadata about how big a 
dynamic allocation is (473 Topic)
• Grows from low addresses to high addresses
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
0x00000000
0xFFFFFFFF
OS kernel [protected]
stack (automatic)
read/write segment
.data, .bss (static)
read-only segment
.text, .rodata (static)
heap (dynamic)

Devic
Devic
The heap, at a glance
• Ultimately the kernel is responsible 
for allocating memory to a process
• Every program begins with a certain 
amount of memory in its heap
• The top of the heap is known 
and the program break.
• The program break gets moved up 
and down as memory is allocated 
and deallocated from the heap.
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
0x00000000
0xFFFFFFFF
OS kernel [protected]
stack (automatic)
read/write segment
.data, .bss (static)
read-only segment
.text, .rodata (static)
heap (dynamic)

Devic
Devic
The heap, at a glance
• Ultimately the kernel is responsible 
for allocating memory to a process
• Every program begins with a certain 
amount of memory in its heap
• The top of the heap is known 
and the program break.
• The program break gets moved up 
and down as memory is allocated 
and deallocated from the heap.
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
0x00000000
0xFFFFFFFF
OS kernel [protected]
stack (automatic)
read/write segment
.data, .bss (static)
read-only segment
.text, .rodata (static)
heap (dynamic)

Devic
Devic
brk() and sbrk() System Calls
• The functions are system calls to request a change in program break
• void* brk( void* ptr ) – requests the program break to be set at 
the address ptr
• This is an absolute pointer, so very dangerous
• Calling brk(NULL) returns the current program break
• void* sbrk( int inc ) – requests the program break to increment
inc bytes upwards or downwards 
• A positive inc allocates new memory
• A negative inc frees memory
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
These functions are declared in the system library unistd.h

Devic
Devic
Are we going to use these calls?
• In short, no.
• In this class we will not directly call these system calls, we will use a system library
• In 473, yes.
• You will implement your own dynamic memory allocation library which directly uses 
these system calls
• Then why tell us?
• The libraries we will use make these calls for us; they are wrappers / abstractions.
• Important to understand that you are still making system calls!
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Dynamic Memory Allocation in C
• So how do we actually request memory dynamically from our operating 
system in C?
• Using the following system library in stdlib.h
 
 
void* malloc(size_t size);
 
 
void* calloc(size_t nmemb, size_t size);
 
 
void* realloc(void* ptr, size_t size)
 
 
void  free(void* ptr);
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
C and calloc
• When you use malloc, the memory is uninitialized, contains garbage
• If you want to zero-out the memory, you can alternatively use calloc
• Returns an array of nmemb members, each of size bytes
• Memory is zeroed out (all bytes have the value 0x0)
• Performance hit, but preferred for non-performance-critical code
void* calloc(size_t nmemb, size_t bytes);
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
// allocate a 10 element int array initialized to 0
int* arr = (int*)calloc(10, sizeof(int));
if (arr == NULL) return -1;
arr[0..9] == 0; // all true

Devic
Devic
Memory Operations
• Given that malloc and calloc return raw memory pointers, it is useful to treat 
these regions as byte-element buffers (when left uncast)
• Given two raw memory addresses, we can perform byte-wise memory 
operations:
• Initialize memory byte-wise to something (set all buffer bytes to be 0x3) 
• Compare memory byte-wise (is buffer 1 == buffer 2)
• Copy memory byte-wise (set buffer 1 = buffer 2)
• Some of this should conceptually be similar to what you’ve already done in 
homework / used in other standard libraries (such as string.h)
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
• We often want to compare buffers to see if they match
• And sometimes also to see if one is byte-wise smaller or larger
• memcmp compares first n bytes of buffers
• The comparison function returns:
• A negative integer if buf1 is less than buf2
• Zero if buf1 is equal to buf2
• A positive integer is buf1 greater than buf2
Comparing memory
int memcmp(void* buf1, void* buf2, size_t n);
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
memcpy(dest, src, n)
is kinda like 
dest = src

Devic
Devic
Realloc (re-allocation)
• realloc changes a previous allocation (resizes it)
• Resizes the previous allocation in place, if possible
• Can be resized larger, or smaller!
• If it can’t resize in place, it will create a new allocation and copy data 
for you
• Essentially does:
• new_allocation = malloc(new_size)
• memcpy(new_allocation, old_allocation, new_size)
• returns NULL if the memory could not be allocated
void *realloc(void *ptr, size_t size)
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Back to dynamic memory
• We have looked at allocating memory dynamically with malloc, calloc, realloc
• And alternatively using some memory functions to perform operations on such
• But now how do we tell the operating system 
we are done with said memory?
• And what would happen if we never did?
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Dangling Pointers
• When a pointer points to a memory address that is accessible (non-NULL) but not 
valid (unallocated/maintained by someone else) it is said that the pointer is a 
dangling pointer
• Why is this an issue?
• What would happen if you started to use 
a pointer after it has been freed?
• It could have been allocated to some 
other dynamic call!
• Solution? Use NULL pointers!
• Recall that (1) any attempt to deference NULL causes a segmentation fault 
• And that (2) free(NULL) does nothing and is safe/ignored
• You should always NULL a pointer after you have free’d it
• it’s better to have a segfault than to corrupt memory! (MEMORY ERRORS CAN BE SILENT!)
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
long* arr = calloc(10*sizeof(long));
if (arr == NULL) return -1;
// .. do something ..
free(arr);
arr = NULL;

Devic
Devic
Memory Safety Concerns
• There are several things you need to watch out for when dealing with memory in C 
(usually dealing with dynamic memory but not always)
• Out of bounds (OOB) errors (arrays/pointers/arithmetic)
• You’ve seen this already more or less…
• Use after free (UAF) (dereferencing a dangling pointer that was not NULL’d)
• Always NULL your pointer after free / do not reuse the pointer/reference/variable
• Double free (DF) (freeing a dangling pointer that was previously already freed)
• Always NULL your pointer after free / do not reuse the pointer/reference/variable
• Invalid free (IF) (freeing a non-dynamic memory pointer (such as a stack variable))
• You can’t avoid using free; Not freeing allocated variables in time is problematic / antithetical
• “Memory leaks”
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Implications of a leak?
• A program’s virtual memory will keep growing
• for short-lived programs, this might be OK
• for long-lived programs, this:
• might slow down over time (caching issues, swap space, etc…)
• might exhaust all available memory and crash (OOM)
• other programs might get starved of memory (silent denial of service)
• in some cases, you might prefer to leak memory than to corrupt memory 
with a buggy free()********* 
• you’re not far enough in programming yet to make this call… ☺
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
How to avoid memory leaks
• You can ask a very simple question to avoid memory leaks.
• Who is responsible for this memory?
• If you are passing a malloc’d pointer back to a caller, then it is the caller’s 
responsibility to free or call a free-like method
• If you are NOT passing a reference to the malloc’d pointer back to the caller, then it is 
YOUR responsibility to free the memory before your function ends.
• Not too complex
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Finding Memory Safety Issues
• GCC has some static memory safety checks
• Address sanitization is a fast instrumentation tool within GCC
• gcc –fsanitize=address foo.c –o foo
• Will tell you about OOB, UAF, DF, and some others when you run the program!
• GDB can inspect memory and use watch points to find issues
• Valgrind is a purpose-built debugger 
for identifying memory leaks and issues
• More heavyweight than GCC’s address sanitizer
• valgrind –v ––leak-check=full ––errors-for-leak-kinds=all ./a.out
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Memory Safety, In Short
• Always check the return status of malloc/calloc/realloc calls
• Always NULL pointers after they are freed
• Understand who is responsible to free memory
• Match every malloc with exactly one free!
• When in doubt, document with comments!
• Free in reverse order of allocation
• Useful for nested structures (hint hint)
• Use the heap for dynamic or large objects, use the stack for small/temporary
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming

Devic
Devic
Summary: dynamic memory interface
 
void *malloc(size_t size);
 
void free(void *ptr);
 
void *calloc(size_t nmemb, size_t size);
 
void *realloc(void *ptr, size_t size);
• These are user-space libraries wrapping system calls. 
• You can write one yourself. (And you will in 473)
• Other commercial allocators:
• dlmalloc (Doug Lea’s malloc – you are using its derivative)
• tcmalloc (thread-caching malloc by Google)
• jemalloc (Jason Evan’s malloc – used by FreeBSD)
CMPSC 311 - Introduction to Systems Programming
